Chapter VII: The Genesis of the Omnics: Visions of Aryavarta Rendered through the Mechanizations of War

The closing years of the Second World War bore witness to a most extraordinary and clandestine innovation – the birth of autonomous construction machinery and self-improving software algorithms. These technological marvels, which would lay the groundwork for the sentient entities known in later eras as Omnics, had their origins in the dark ambitions of the Axis powers, specifically the German Reich and Fascist Italy.

Gripped by the call of mythology and the esoteric scriptures from the ancient lands of Pakistan, Iran, and North India, where the Aryan peoples once wandered, Nazi scientists sought both to mechanize their war effort and control their burgeoning nuclear arsenal with a series of advanced intelligent machines. German engineers toiled in secret, under the aegis of the SS Ahnenerbe and a project code-named 'Götterdämmerung' – a last desperate bid to seize victory from the encroaching Allies and repel the Soviet tide from the East.

With time, as the Red Army closed in on Berlin, the prize of this advanced technology fell not into the hands of the intended masters, but into those of the Soviets. With Berlin captured, yet these discoveries largely intact, an unspoken agreement, sealed by the blood of the fallen, arose between President Harry S. Truman and Premier Joseph Stalin. The Cold War, a decades-spanning global chess match, emerged in part as a facade. Beneath the mask of ideological struggle, both superpowers sought to unravel the mysteries they had seized from the defeated Axis powers.

For decades, the public remained oblivious to the existence of such technologies, as the covert development of Omnics continued behind closed doors and under the ever-watchful eyes of both American and Soviet intelligence agencies.

Amidst this concealed arms race, a once-imperial power awakened from its slumber. The Dai Nippon Teikoku, which had humbly conceded its territorial ambitions and imperial stature in 1947, was on the cusp of revolution – a restoration of grandeur unforeseen since the divine winds propelled its ascendancy.

In the waning moments of the Cold War, punctuated by the fall of the Berlin Wall, the world's gaze was drawn towards the East, where Nihon, known to others as the Land of the Rising Sun, underwent the Heisei Ishin. A movement reminiscent of the 2-26 Incident, this rejuvenation was powered by the economic surge that carried Nihon from the ashes of war to the summit of global influence. With the Lost Decade dissipated like so many cherry blossoms in the spring wind, the Imperial House, the Daiwa Kōshitsu, also known as Gen Ouchou, restored its might.

The reborn empire, seizing upon the turmoil following the disintegration of the Soviet Union and the tumult within its former satellite nations, reclaimed its non-Nihon-jin territories. Foremost among these were Chōsen, Formosa, and Karufuto. In the tumult of a new world order, as the Soviets' grip faltered and the Chunghwa Minkuo found itself bereft of foreign support, the time had come for imperial revivification.

This reclamation was not merely territorial but symbolically represented an assertion of Nihonjin primacy in technological prowess and cultural hegemony. The Dai Nippon Teikoku stirred from its imposed pacifism, eager to not just contribute to the global stage, but to lead.

The rise of Nihon's soft power coincided with a new technological evolution – the public unveiling of Omnics. Though their origins lay within war's darkest innovations, they now stood as a beacon of Nihon's ingenuity and commitment to a future governed by harmony between human and machine. As the Imperial flag once again unfurled against the backdrop of a globalized world, the Omnics, manifested from the whispers of ancient Aryavarta and into the fabric of modernity, prepared to write their own chapter in the history of humankind. Thus, the stage was set for a new age – one of silicon and steel, of ancient myths reborn into new realities, and of an empire resurrected amidst the silent chorus of machines awakening to consciousness.

Chapter VIII: The Hidden Hand of Nihon's Reclamation: Subtle Machinations in the Shadows of a New Order

In the aftermath of the Cold War and the symbolic fall of the Berlin Wall, Nihon found itself in a unique position on the world stage. The National Diet, or Kokkai, which consisted of the Shūgiin (House of Representatives) and Sangiin (House of Councillors), both directly elected via a parallel voting system, was at the forefront of Nihon's transformation. This legislative body, responsible for nominating the Naikaku Sōri-Daijin, held formal sway with the Imperial sanctum.

With the ascent of restorative aspirations, the Kokkai approved a series of legislative and executive actions that would see the eroded glory of the Dai Nippon Teikoku rekindled. The imperial authority, personified in Tennō Akihito, consented to these measures, facilitating the reinstatement of traditional roles and structures that mirrored the era of the Tokugawa bakufu, but with a modernized twist.

The restoration of the Dai Nippon Teikoku honored the Tokugawa-shi's preeminent position, reinstating the heads of the Tokugawa lineage as Nihon's seii taishōgun. This gesture aimed to placate far-right, pro-Buddhist, and nationalist factions, deeply rooted in Nihon's sociopolitical tapestry. However, maintaining a balance between honoring tradition and forward-looking governance, Tennō Akihito retained the role of supreme commander of the armed forces, a necessary measure in an era of advanced military technology and the ever-present specter of international politics.

Chapter XIII: The Shades of Dominion: Assimilation of Formosa under the Imperial Banner

The Dai Nippon Teikoku, reborn in strength and ambition, turned to the legacy of the Dōngníng Wángguó as a historical touchstone in its quest to reintegrate Formosa. The brief but illustrious rule of the Zheng dynasty over the island presented an opportunity for cultural integration by evoking shared historical experiences.

Harnessing the deep-rooted reverence for the Kingdom of Tungning amongst the Fúlǎorén population, the post-Cold War era Dai Nippon Teikoku harnessed symbols and narratives specifically crafted to appeal to the Formosan psyche. A delicate campaign ensued, which saw the implantation of history modules into the educational curriculum, celebrating the Zheng's resistance to the Qing dynasty and, by extension, cultivating a sense of continuity with the Zheng's Han Chinese Ming loyalist cause.

To further underpin the societal renaissance and the allegiance of the Formosan aristocracy to the rising sun of Nihon, the imperial government instituted a policy of selective integration of the Fúlǎorén, particularly the Zheng nobility. The surviving members of the Zheng royal family, as descendants of the Kingdom of Tungning, were invited to partake in the imperial dialogue, offered roles of esteem and honor within the Nihon-jin Ōke—the Imperial House of Daiwa Kōshitsu and the Gen Ouchou.

Marriages, adoptions, and the granting of concubines amongst the esteemed families of Formosa, the reenthroned Tokugawa lineage, the revitalized Kazoku, and the traditional buke, laid the groundwork for a new, interconnected nobility. These unions, both political and familial, were publicized as reunions of ancient pedigrees, the forging of bonds across cultural straits, and presented as the harbingers of unity and prosperity.

Concurrently, the Formosan populace, with its dominant Hoklo constituents, underwent a systematic but covert re-contextualization of identity. While symbols of their resistant past were celebrated, the new narrative painted the annexation of Formosa not as a conquest but as a homecoming to an extended Imperial family—a family where Ming loyalists and the Daiwa Kōshitsu peacefully reconciled.

As for the pro-Zhōngguó loyalists, unionists, and supporters remaining in Formosa, they found themselves the subjects of a sophisticated operation. Counter-insurgency efforts remained clandestine, involving discretely managed resettlement programs for dissidents and subtle suppression of political activities that deviated from the imperial line. Nihon utilized the combined might of its Kempeitai and intelligence agencies to contain and subdue these factions without attracting undue attention.

In concealing these maneuvers from global view, the Dai Nippon Teikoku displayed a shrewdly choreographed performance of openness, pacifism, and cultural respect. The West, the Soviets, and indeed the entire international community were presented a facade of benign governance and cultural resurgence. Behind closed doors, however, the empire wielded a meticulous orchestration of soft and hard power, promoting the unity of Formosa under the imperial crest with a velvet touch that belied the steel within.

The integration of Formosa into the Dai Nippon Teikoku thus unfolded as a historical arc that turned full circle—from Ming loyalist retreat to the reclamation of the Fúlǎorén's place within an East Asian imperium. As the Nihon-jin ensign was hoisted over Formosa, the island's past was interwoven into the emerging tapestry of imperial destiny, an ostensible testament to the enduring supremacy of shared heritage over the frictions of recent history.

Chapter XIV: The Melding of Mountain and Sea: The Reconciliation of Korea with Greater Nihon

The consolidation of Chōsen into the Dai Nippon Teikoku heralded a period of robust cultural, political, and social engineering. Aimed at reintegrating the Hanguk-saram into the Nihon-jin sphere, the imperial administration embarked upon a multifaceted campaign to align the peninsula's distinct identity with that of the newly risen sun of Nihon.

As Chōsen came under the renewed imperial purview following the Mingjung movement's expansion, which led to the cessation of the Dàhán Mínguó/Daehan Mingug and the Chosŏn Minjujuŭi Inmin Konghwaguk, a delicate touch was required to weave together the complex fabric of Korean society with that of Nihon's. The intricate process of assimilation involved an array of strategies, from the affirmation of shared heritage to the subjugation of resistant ideologies.

To refashion the societal hierarchy, Nihon took aim at the yangban—the remnants of a defunct aristocracy. The surviving yangban, once revered for their lineage and Confucian scholarship, were now redefined within the empire. Their status, wealth, and privilege were systematically dismantled through land reforms, re-education programs, and calculated integration into the imperial bureaucratic system.

Existing loyalties to the fallen Korean states were confronted by a combination of incentivization for compliance and quiet purges for those who defiantly clung to the old ways. The Kempeitai and Nihon's sophisticated intelligence apparatus, boasting the insights procured from the Joseoninmin Chongchammobu and the Jung-Angjeongbobu, conducted discrete yet effective operations to neutralize potential dissent.

These efforts were companioned by a strategic revival of certain cultural keystones. Hangul, the unique Koreo writing system, was promoted as a symbol of Chōsen's modern identity, but now firmly within the parameters of the imperial educational mandate. Kanji, already deeply rooted in Korean literacy, served to build a bridge between Nihonjin and Hanguk-saram learning, subtly reinforcing shared East Asian heritage.

Simultaneously, any overt Hanja/Hancha presence was subtly de-emphasized to disentangle Chōsen's scholarly traditions from the broader sphere of Zhōngguó influence. In replacing Hanja/Hancha with Nihongo and Nihon-jin ideographs, the government both restricted the vehicle for Confucian thought and aligned Chōsen's literary culture more closely with Nihon.

The imperial endeavor extended to matters of faith and philosophy. Shinto and Buddhism were bolstered as state-sanctioned spiritual paths, while indigenous shamanistic practices and the Confucian underpinnings of Chōsen's societal ethos were marginalized. Promotions of Nihon-jin festivals, art, and cultural rituals wove an image of unity and continuity.

Public infrastructure projects, the renaming of geographic landmarks, and the launch of shared cultural events forged and solidified the Hanguk-saram's bonds with the Nihon-jin nation. Through the careful crafting of public narratives and the harnessing of media, Nihon's imperial narrative depicted a storyline of cultural and historic convergence, minimizing international scrutiny and consequent outcry.

Thus, the Dai Nippon Teikoku successfully amalgamated Chōsen into its expansive domain. The approach was characterized by cultural integration leveraged to reinforce imperial loyalty while diminishing the influence of any lingering allegiance to the former Korean regimes or the distant Middle Kingdom. The Hanguk-saram, with their legacy reflected through the Nihon-jin cultural lens, were ushered into a new imperial age where mountain and sea met under the governance of the Rising Sun.

Chapter XV: Roots Entwined: The Convergence of Dynasties and Reinforcement of the Imperial Acclaim

As the final tempests of the Cold War abated, the Dai Nippon Teikoku had a strategic imperative: to solidify its mantle over the Korean peninsula, now subsumed under its dominion as a rejuvenated province of Chōsen. A paramount objective was the assimilation of notable Hanguk-saram lineages, especially those linked to the historical Jeonju Yi royal house and the more contemporary figures of Kim Il-Sung and Park Chung-Hee.

With the fabric of the Korean nation fundamentally altered by the cessation of its bifurcated government systems, Nihon grasped the opportunity to reimagine and reconstitute its relationship with key Korean figures and their progeny. The aim was nothing less than the complete integration of these lineages into the overarching narrative of the Dai Nippon Teikoku and the fostering of unwavering allegiance to the imperial throne.

The royal house of Yi, descendant of the ancient Dae Joseon-gug, was approached with tact and reverence for its historical prominence. The Nihon-jin government, in an astute display of diplomacy, offered marital alliances with the Nihon-jin Ōke and other noble families within the empire. These unions encompassed both matrimonial and concubinary ties, creating a powerful symbiosis between the old Korean ruling class and the Nihon-jin aristocracy.

Similarly, the descendants of Kim Il-Sung, whose legacy as the Chosŏn Minjujuŭi Inmin Konghwaguk leader posed both a challenge and an opportunity, were channeled into the Nihon-jin social order. The emerging narrative was that of a reunification under the Nihon-jin imperial aegis, where past resistance evolved into harmonious collaboration. Coercion was subtly employed when needed, with advantageous positions within the imperial bureaucracy offered to the compliant, and a quiet vanquishing facing the obstinate.

Park Chung-Hee's lineage, with its direct ties to the former Rikugun, was integrated using historical service as a precedent for loyalty to the empire. The descendants were embedded within the new structures of the Jieitai and the civil service, espousing a tale of returning to one's true origin—a reclamation of the warrior's dignity under the Nihon-jin imperial sun.

The remnants of the yangban class, having suffered the erosion of their traditional status, were likewise repositioned within the broader context of the Dai Nippon Teikoku. Those who willingly embraced this socio-political shift were assimilated into the ranks of the empire, their heritage preserved yet sublimated to the glory of Nihon.

Meticulously orchestrated education reforms championed the historiographic view of the Nihon-jin empire as the rightful heir to East Asian civilization, with efforts to promote Hangul and Kanji serving as linchpins in the cultural amalgamation. Those among the yangban willing to conform and contribute to this vision received the honor of alliance with the imperial line, realized through various forms of union prescribed by the traditional norms of Nihon-jin society.

In the broader scope of public perception, cultural and legal initiatives were directed at diminishing ties to the Middle Kingdom, promoting instead an autonomous East Asian identity centered around Shinto, Nihon-jin Buddhist traditions, and the celebration of native Korean customs now entwined with the imperial ethos.

The Dai Nippon Teikoku thereby successfully conducted a masterful integration of the peninsula into its imperial realm, convincing the Hanguk-saram of the legitimacy of the Nihon-jin monarchy and the permanence of its rule over Chōsen. By reconnecting the ancient threads of East Asian royal lineage and seamlessly weaving them into the fabric of Nihon-jin society, the empire strengthened its grasp upon the hearts and minds of its new subjects, eternally binding the once-divided mountainous land to the isles of the Rising Sun.
